coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized multiple fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as an efficient label-free optical nanoprobe for in vivo live-cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Clament Sagaya Selvam, N.
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have been synthesized successfully by a simple hydrothermal method and demonstrated its application for multicolor imaging in Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) as an in vivo model. The synthesized N-CQDs were characterized by various physicochemical techniques such as XRD, Raman spectroscopy, ATR-FTIR spectroscopy, XPS, HRTEM, UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited a strong fluorescence due to the uniform size distribution with nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups onto the surface of N-CQDs which induce the excellent dispersibility in aqueous media. The N-CQDs has an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior and the strongest fluorescence appeared at 411 nm (emission peak position) under the excitation of 340 nm. Also, the N-CQDs displayed a high quantum yield (QY) of 12.5. The fluorescence behaviour of the aqueous N-CQDs suspension retains for a long time up to 1 year. The prolonging fluorescent N-CQDs was utilized as a staining agent for bioimaging and toxicity of N-CQDs on C. elegans that was conducted by killing assay. In-vivo studies suggested that the N-CQDs displayed excellent biocompatibility and successfully used for high-contrast imaging of N-CQDs in living and dead C. elegans. Based on the strongest fluorescence along with excellent aqueous dispersibility and biocompatibility, the green synthesized N-CQDs would be an ideal candidate for many biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phyllanthus acidus
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Tunable fluorescence
Caenorhabditis elegans
Biocompatibility
Live-cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurements of the quantum yield of N-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ) and the Priority Research Centers Program ( 2014R1A6A1031189 ). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government ( 2017R1C1B5076345 ). We would like to thank Dr. Vinothkannan Ravichandran, State Key Laboratory for Microbial Technology, Shandong University, China for preparing the C. elegans and fluorescence imaging experiment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit.
(a) Thermogravimetric curve and (b) ATR-FTIR spectrum of the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C1s spectrum, (c) N1s spectrum and (d) O1s spectrum of N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit.
(a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) particle size distribution and (d) SAED pattern of the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) fluorescence excitation spectrum, (c) fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and (d) the respective normalized fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of the aqueous solution of N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit. The inset shows the photographic-optical images of the aqueous solution of N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit under daylight and UV light.
(a) Optical photographs under UV light as well as daylight and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of the aqueous N-CQDs solution at 1st and 365th days.

(a) Toxicity assay of C. elegans incubation with N-CQDs at different concentrations prepared from P. acidus fruit. Multicolor in vivo model C. elegans incubation with N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit imaging under the excitation of (b) 400 nm, (c) 470 nm, (d) 550 nm, (e) bright field (BF) and (f) merge (overlap). Live C. elegans were immobilized using 0.05% sodium azide (NaN3) for imaging under fluorescence filters. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Multicolor in vivo model dead C. elegans incubation with N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit imaging under the excitation of (a) 400 nm, (b) 470 nm, (c) 550 nm and (d) bright field (BF). (For interpretation of the references to colour in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Green synthesized multiple fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as an efficient label-free optical nanoprobe for in vivo live-cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University, Suwon 16419, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have been synthesized successfully by a simple hydrothermal method and demonstrated its application for multicolor imaging in Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) as an in vivo model. The synthesized N-CQDs were characterized by various physicochemical techniques such as XRD, Raman spectroscopy, ATR-FTIR spectroscopy, XPS, HRTEM, UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited a strong fluorescence due to the uniform size distribution with nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups onto the surface of N-CQDs which induce the excellent dispersibility in aqueous media. The N-CQDs has an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior and the strongest fluorescence appeared at 411 nm (emission peak position) under the excitation of 340 nm. Also, the N-CQDs displayed a high quantum yield (QY) of 12.5. The fluorescence behaviour of the aqueous N-CQDs suspension retains for a long time up to 1 year. The prolonging fluorescent N-CQDs was utilized as a staining agent for bioimaging and toxicity of N-CQDs on C. elegans that was conducted by killing assay. In-vivo studies suggested that the N-CQDs displayed excellent biocompatibility and successfully used for high-contrast imaging of N-CQDs in living and dead C. elegans. Based on the strongest fluorescence along with excellent aqueous dispersibility and biocompatibility, the green synthesized N-CQDs would be an ideal candidate for many biological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the new family of nanomaterials and it has been considerable attention since their discovery [1] due to their favorable optical properties, chemical stability, and low toxicity [2,3]. Based on the outstanding properties of CQDs, it has been successfully used in the field of biomedicine research, such as drug delivery, as a powerful fluorescent probe bio-imaging, fluorescent ink, sensing, and photocatalysis [4–12]. Remarkable efforts have been made on the synthesis of CQDs with outstanding optical properties using various precursors and methods, such as laser ablation, arc discharge, solvothermal oxidation, hydrothermal treatment, microwave irradiation and so on associated with synthetic and natural carbon precursors [13–16]. Among them, the combination hydrothermal associated through natural sources like bio-waste and plant resources can provide advantages in terms of significantly reducing production cost, simplified strategy, easy available raw materials, and low toxicity [17,18]. In addition, the doping of heteroatoms like nitrogen (N) into the CQDs (N-CQDs) considerably increase the optical properties including quantum yield, photostability, and fluorescence ability. Hence, the green synthesized N-CQDs are considered to replace conventionally used fluorescence probes, which was more suitable for many novel applications [19,20]. One of the important applications of N-CQDs is bioimaging. N-CQDs have ideal optical properties including different emission colors and they can be used for the detection of targeted cells in living animals. Using internal injection of N-CQDs nanoprobes, they obtained sensitive and multicolor fluorescence imaging of targeted cells under in vivo conditions. In vivo studies afforded possibilities for ultrasensitive and multiplexed imaging of molecular targets in the real biological systems [13,21].

In the present work, the Phyllanthus acidus (P. acidus) as a carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as an N-dopant have been utilized to prepare a tunable fluorescent N-CQDs. The obtained N-CQDs shows a good water dispersibility, brightest multiple fluorescences and low toxicity which potentially applied as an optical nanoprobe in live-cell imaging without any surface passivation. Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) were used as an in vivo model to check the toxicity and fluorescence imaging of N-CQDs.

P. acidus fruits were procured from Dharmapuri, Tamilnadu, India. Aqueous ammonia (25%) and sodium azide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Republic of Korea. All the chemicals were used as received. The Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this work.

N-CQDs was synthesized using the juice of the P. acidus fruit by a simple hydrothermal method as per our earlier report without major modifications [22]. Typically, 30 mL of the P. acidus fruit juice was taken into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with this addition of 1 mL of aqueous ammonia. The autoclave was tightly closed and subsequently, the mixture was heated at 200 °C for 12 h in a hot air oven. After that, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The final brown solution was filtered through a mixed cellulose esters membrane filter with the pore size of 0.22 μm. The solid residue was discarded and the filtrate (supernatant) was collected which denoted as nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The obtained N-CQDs were stored in a refrigerator at 5 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of the quantum yield of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized N-CQDs was calculated based on an established procedure [23–25]. In brief, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QYR is 0.54) was used as the reference. The QY was calculated according to the following Eq. (1):
(1)
QY = Q
Y R
I S
A R
n S
2
I R
A S
n R
2
where, “I” is the measured integrated fluorescent emission intensity, “n” is the refractive index of the solvent, and “A” is the absorbance (intensity). The subscript “R” and “S” refers to the corresponding parameter of the known fluorescent reference standard and for the synthesized sample, respectively.

The synthesized N-CQDs were characterized by various physicochemical techniques. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out using a PANalytical X’Pert3 MRD diffractometer with monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA and were recorded in the range from 10 to 90° (2θ). The dry dark blackish-brown powder was filled in an XRD glass holder and was then measured in reflection geometry. Raman spectrum was recorded on a Thermo Scientific DXR SmartRaman spectrometer with the range from 50 to 3500 cm−1. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the material was performed using a TA Instruments SDT-Q600 analyzer. Samples of approximately 3 mg were heated in a nitrogen atmosphere (100 mL min–1) from 20 (room temperature) to 1000 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min–1. Attenuated total reflection–Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra were recorded in transmittance mode on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum Two in the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm−1 by the addition of 16 scans at a resolution of 16 cm−1. A small amount of powder sample was placed on the ATR set-up for the analyses. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were achieved using a K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific). CasaXPS software was used for the deconvolution of the high-resolution XPS spectra. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed with an FEI-Tecnai TF-20 transmission electron microscope with an operating accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The sample was prepared on carbon-coated copper grids by drop-casting of aqueous N-CQDs and dried at room temperature to evaporate the water for capturing of HRTEM. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded from 200 to 800 nm using an OPTIZEN 3220UV spectrophotometer. Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excitation wavelength was varied to determine the maximum emission intensity was achieved by varying the excitation wavelength. The slit width was fixed at 5 nm and the scan speed was set to 240 nm/min.

Toxicity analysis of the lead compound was performed, for this C. elegans (approximately 60 worms per well of 98 well non-binding plates, Corning™, USA) were counted and suspended in S-basal buffer. Each well plate was then treated with various concentrations of N-CQDs (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175 and 200 μg mL−1), following which the plates were incubated at 25 °C for 48 h. After the incubation period, the worms (C. elegans) were scrutinized and observed for dead worms by prodding with a platinum wire gauge. All experiments were performed in three replicates.

Staining and imaging assays were done as follows; the worms (live and/or dead) in S-basal buffer were collected and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was removed, and the pellet containing the worms (nematodes) were stained with N-CQDs (50 μg mL−1) and incubated for 24 h at room temperature (25 °C). The excess staining solution (N-CQDs) was removed by further centrifugation, and the nematodes were resuspended in S-basal buffer and imaged under a fluorescent microscope at 10X magnification.

In order to determine the crystallinity and graphitization of synthesized N-CQDs, the XRD analysis was performed and XRD pattern of the N-CQDs made from the juice of P. acidus fruit is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The XRD pattern shows a major broad reflection centered at 23.1° (2θ) and minor reflection centered around 42.3° (2θ) which is corresponding to the typical graphite reflection of (002) and (100) planes, respectively [26]. Moreover, the broad reflection is due to the small size of the obtained N-CQDs. Lattice space of (002) plane for N-CQDs is 0.38 nm which is close to the graphite (002) lattice spacing. The obtained lattice space of N-CQDs (0.38 nm) is larger than that of typical bulk graphite (0.34 nm), indicating partial crystallization/moderate degree of graphitization [27]. The partial degree of crystallization/graphitization of N-CQDs due to partial turbostratic carbon structure and also because of densely packed oxygen-bearing and nitrogen-bearing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. To understand the structure of N-CQDs, the Raman spectroscopy was performed and the Raman spectrum of N-CQDs is presented in Fig. 1(b). The spectrum display characteristic weaker D-band at 1354 cm−1 and stronger G-bands at 1584 cm−1. The D-band is associated with defects in the lattice of graphite (sp3-hybridized carbon networks) and D-band is related to graphitic nature of carbon framework (sp2-hybridized carbon networks) [28]. The ratio of the intensities of the D-band to G-band (ID/IG) is commonly used to express the degree of graphitization of graphene-like carbon materials. The ID/IG ratio of the N-CQDs was 0.75 indicating that relatively acceptable degree of graphitization due to the there are some defects in the sp2-hybridized carbon lattice of N-CQDs arising from some heteroatoms and organic functional groups on the surface [29]. The results of the Raman spectrum reveals that the oxygen-bearing and nitrogen-contain functional groups are predominantly present over N-CQDs surface which resulting the relatively moderate degree of graphitization of N-CQDs [30,31].

The evidence of the functionalization of synthesized N-CQDs was first obtained from TGA under the nitrogen atmosphere. Fig. 2 (a) shows the TGA curve of the synthesized N-CQDs. The continuous weight loss were observed between the temperature of 20 and 550 °C, the first stage of sudden weight loss (∼18 wt.%) observed up to 200 °C is due to desorption of water molecules which originates from physically adsorbed water molecules and condensation/elimination of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the N-CQDs. The second stage of distinct weight loss (∼32 wt.%) observed between 200 and 550 °C corresponds to the decomposition of stable functional groups includes carboxylic acid groups at the edges of the N-CQDs structure. The huge amount of weight loss (∼50 wt.%) is credited to a vast number of functional groups on the N-CQDs and it was further supported by FTIR spectroscopy and XPS analysis. The final stage of weight loss (∼17 wt.%) beyond 550 °C which is credited to the decomposition of N-CQDs (carbon) skeleton [32,33].

The functionalization of synthesized N-CQDs was further revealed through FTIR spectroscopy analysis. ATR-FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs produced by a one-step hydrothermal process of the juice of P. acidus fruit is showed as Fig. 2(b). In the FTIR transmittance spectrum of the N-CQDs, the broad peaks located around 3250 cm−1 correspond to the OH (hydroxyl) functional groups originated from the fruit juice of P. acidus and or physically adsorbed water molecules on the N-CQDs [34]. The weak absorption bands observed at 2927 and 2860 cm−1 can be assigned to the as-symmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations of the CH groups over the N-CQDs, respectively [35]. The absorption bands positioned at 1710, 1600, 1445, 1370 and 1280–1030 cm−1 correspond to the individual functional groups such as CO (carboxyl), CC, CN, COH, and COC (epoxy) which are present in the resulted N-CQDs [36,37]. The presence of these functional groups on the N-CQDs surface is responsible for their excellent dispersion in aqueous media [38].

The surface functionalization/chemical compositions and elemental states of the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit were examined by XPS. The survey spectrum of the N-CQDs displays three predominant peaks at 284, 400, and 532 eV (Fig. 3 (a)) correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, with corresponding content as 70, 10 and 20% (Inset Fig. 3(a)), respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 3(b)), shows four component peaks after deconvolution with binding energies of about 284.08, 285.08, 286.98 and 290.08 eV that corresponds to CC/CC, CH/COH, CNC/COC, OCOH functional groups, respectively [39]. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the N1s peak was deconvoluted into three components centered at 397.98, 399.08 and 401.38 eV reflects the bonding structure of CNC, NH and (C)3–N, respectively which verifies the synthesized carbon materials having the nitrogen substituents on the surface [40,41]. The O1s region (Fig. 3(d)) had three components with binding energies of about 530.28, 531.68 and 533.98 eV for COH/COC, CO and COH/HOH functional groups, respectively [42]. Thus, the XPS spectra indicated that the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit were rich in hydrophilic functional groups such as COOH, OH, and NH on the surfaces, which is consistent with the FTIR results.

The morphology and size of the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit were examined by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the N-CQDs are nearly spherical in shape with excellent distributions. Moreover, the HRTEM image (Fig. 4(b)) displays the clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, which is assigned to the (100) facet of graphitic (sp2) carbon [43,44] and outer part of the N-CQDs is poor graphitic nature. Hence, the HRTEM image reveals that the N-CQDs exhibit an acceptable/partial graphitic structure. Fig. 4(c) shows the size distribution of corresponding TEM image (Fig. 4(a)), the size distribution graphs clearly indicate narrow size distribution of N-CQDs with a diameter about 5 nm which match well with the Gaussian distribution fit. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit are shown in Fig. 4(d). This pattern indicates the partial crystalline/graphitic nature of the N-CQDs which might be due to the introduction of more oxygen-containing/nitrogen-containing groups on their surface.

The optical properties such as UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission of the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit were confirmed through UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 5 (a) represent the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-CQDs, two broad absorption bands at 272 and 320 nm correlated to the typical π → π* and n → π* transitions, respectively [45]. The broad absorption might be credited to the complicated band structure and random energy levels of the N-CQDs [46]. The UV–vis spectroscopy results suggest that the presence of oxygen-containing/nitrogen-containing functional groups and CC sp2 carbon domains appeared in the N-CQDs. The bright blue fluorescence of the aqueous N-CQDs solution under the illumination of UV-light (λex = 365 nm) is shown as the inset of Fig. 5(a). Nevertheless, the aqueous N-CQDs solution displays a pale yellow color under the illumination of daylight. The fluorescence excitation spectrum exhibited in Fig. 5(b) and displayed an intense band at 340 nm correlated to maximum excitation for the strongest/brightest fluorescence. The fluorescence emission spectra under variable excitation wavelengths for N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 5(c). The fluorescence emission spectra display a redshift with the increment of the excitation wavelength. In addition, the fluorescence intensity increased with increasing the excitation wavelength and then decreased. Prepared N-CQDs from P. acidus fruit show the strongest fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of about 340 nm. The normalized fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. 5(d)) clearly exhibit redshift by increasing the excitation wavelength (320–480 nm). The origin of the optical properties of N-CQDs is still ardently discussed and not yet well understood. Therefore, the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission modulation resulting from the size of the N-CQDs, amino edge functionalization, surface traps and formation of aromatic structures [47–49]. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibits tunable fluorescence, thus, it is more suitable for multicolor cell imaging.

Long-term fluorescence stability in aqueous is one of the crucial factors for the optical materials. Hence, the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit was dispersed in DI water, subsequently, the fluorescence properties were measured immediately and after 1 year (365 days) stored aqueous N-CQDs solution to check the prolonging stability of the N-CQDs. Fig. 6 shows the optical-photographs and fluorescence emission spectra of the aqueous N-CQDs solution at different storage time (1st and 365th days). The aqueous solution of N-CQDs displays a pale yellow and brightest blue fluorescence under the daylight and UV light, respectively (Fig. 6(a)). The brightest blue fluorescence of aqueous N-CQDs solution retains without major changes even after 365 days of storage, suggesting the aqueous N-CQDs solution exhibits prolong stability. Furthermore, the prolong-stability of aqueous N-CQDs solution confirmed by spectroscopy technique. The fluorescence emission intensity of the aqueous N-CQDs solution at 365th days shows insignificant decrement compared to the 1st day. The result strongly suggests that the aqueous N-CQDs solution has prolonged stability which could be suitable for optical applications including sensors and medical imaging.

C. elegans were used as an in vivo model to check the toxicity of N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit. Worm (nematode/C. elegans) killing assays were performed as per established protocols [50]. Interestingly, N-CQDs did not have any detrimental effect on C. elegans and even at the highest concentration (200 μg mL−1), approximately 93% worms survived (Fig. 7 (a)), and also revealed the regular phenotypic traits. Thus, the N-CQDs is nontoxic to a eukaryotic model implies its potential use for alternate biological procedures. Further, the staining efficacy of N-CQDs on adult worms were checked. Results show that N-CQDs are readily diffusible and thus show intense fluorescence inside the worms. The fluorescence intensities were uniformly distributed within the worm’s body and multiple fluorescences were confirmed by blue (400 nm), green (450 nm), and red (550 nm) fluorescence filters (Fig. 7(b–d)). Importantly, the fluorescence levels were matched in the merged image indicating that N-CQDs might stain the proteins, RNA or DNA and thus shows whole-body fluorescence (Fig. 7(f)). Further experiments are warranted to confirm the residues which play an active role in this observed multiple fluorescences. Furthermore, the N-CQDs prepared from P. acidus fruit used as optical nanoprobes for multiple fluorescence imaging on dead C. elegans as an in vivo model. Initially, the C. elegans was killed by using 0.5% sodium azide. Then, the dead C. elegans was incubated with the N-CQDs as follows, the procedure was followed as performed for live C. elegans. The stained dead C. elegans were conducted for fluorescence imaging experiments under fluorescence microscopy with different fluorescence filters such as blue, green, and red. Fig. 8 shows the in vivo fluorescence imaging of dead C. elegans incubated with N-CQDs. The images display the brightest multiple fluorescences in whole-body by varying the excitation wavelengths (fluorescence filters) which confirms the N-CQDs stained the whole-body of the C. elegans.

In summary, the N-CQDs were prepared from P. acidus fruit as the carbon source and aqueous ammonia as a nitrogen dopant by a one-pot hydrothermal method. The obtained N-CQDs has narrow size distribution with a diameter of about 5 nm. The N-CQDs exhibited excellent aqueous solubility and good optical properties with high QY (12.5). N-CQDs showed low toxicity and good biocompatibility. In addition with their excellent optical properties and good biocompatibility made the N-CQDs promising optical/fluorescence nanoprobes for real-time live-cell imaging. The N-CQDs were intensely stained and multiple fluorescences were uniformly distributed within the whole-body of the nematodes (C. elegans) based on the different fluorescence filters. Moreover, their small size and brightest multicolor fluorescence with good biocompatibility, N-CQDs may potentially used in many biological applications including drug delivery in near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was partially supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675) and the Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189). This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) of Korea government (2017R1C1B5076345). We would like to thank Dr. Vinothkannan Ravichandran, State Key Laboratory for Microbial Technology, Shandong University, China for preparing the C. elegans and fluorescence imaging experiment.
